text: Hello, I'm sharing a robotic cholecystectomy after a percutaneous cholecystostomy done several months earlier in a patient who actually was recovering from a separate surgical procedure when they developed a calculus cholecystitis. Challenges in this case were that the adhesions were very thick and broad rather than thin and flimsy, made the anatomy a little bit more distorted.

The surgeon attempts to take down thick adhesions at the inferior edge of the gallbladder. The anatomy is distorted, making it difficult to distinguish the duodenum and transverse colon. The gallbladder wall becomes visible, aided by ICG fluorescence, which highlights the cystic duct and assists in identifying dissection planes.
The surgeon removes the drain site to increase the mobility of the gallbladder over the liver edge. Indocyanine green (ICG) is visible within the bile, indicating that the cystic duct has reopened.

The surgeon attempts to free adhesions at the bottom of the gallbladder, aiming to dissect within the hepatocystic triangle. The common duct and cystic duct are visible, with the duodenum also in view.

The surgeon attempts to dissect near the bottom of the gallbladder cystic duct, encountering a thick layer of adhesions in the peritoneum. The gallbladder is elevated, causing tenting of the common bile duct, and despite visibility of the cystic duct with ICG, the dissection appears challenging.

The surgeon encounters minor bleeding during the dissection of the gallbladder and decides to switch to a top-down approach due to scarring from a previous tube placement. A sponge is used to control the bleeding and maintain a clear surgical field.
The surgeon continues to detach the gallbladder from the liver using a top-down approach, carefully maneuvering to stay within the proper dissection plane and minimize bleeding.

The surgeon uses ICG to visualize the gallbladder wall and liver bed, aiming to isolate the gallbladder to only the duct and artery attachments. During dissection, feeding vessels are encountered and controlled with bipolar cautery.

The surgeon uses bipolar cautery and scissors to control bleeding while dissecting the junction of the cystic duct and common duct. The gallbladder is mobilized, allowing for inferior dissection to open the enveloping peritoneum at the bottom of the gallbladder, isolating the cystic duct. The cystic duct is clearly visible, and the surgeon prepares to identify the cystic artery.

The surgeon carefully dissects behind the cystic duct, revealing the cystic artery located higher in the triangle than the duct. The dissection progresses with precision to create a clear window behind the cystic duct.

The surgeon uses ICG to assist in the dissection, clearing the area behind the cystic duct to identify the cystic artery. A crossing vessel, identified as one of the hepatic arteries, is visible. The surgeon creates a window behind the cystic artery and clips the artery and duct, with one clip on the artery and two on the duct, achieving hemostasis to conclude the procedure.
